questions.q: What does the file mode rwsr-xr-x on an executable indicate?
options: The owner has set a watchpoint on the file
The setuid bit is set — it runs with the file owner's privileges
The file is sticky and can't be deleted
questions.why: The s in the owner-execute slot is the setuid bit. Common on passwd, sudo, ping. Setuid root binaries are a classic privesc target — audit them with find / -perm -4000.

questions.q: Which file stores hashed user passwords on a modern Linux system?
questions.why: /etc/passwd is world-readable and stores user metadata. /etc/shadow is root-readable only and holds the hashed passwords (yescrypt, sha512crypt, etc.).

questions.q: What does a SUID-set /bin/bash let an attacker do?
options: Run a shell as root if root owns the binary
Nothing — bash drops SUID by default
questions.why: Bash and most shells drop privileges when SUID is set unless invoked with -p. So a naked SUID bash isn't an instant root shell, but bash -p is. GTFOBins documents the real escapes.

questions.q: What does ss -tlnp show?
options: Listening TCP sockets with the owning process
questions.why: -t TCP, -l listening, -n numeric, -p process. ss replaced netstat from the deprecated net-tools package.

questions.q: Where do you usually look first to investigate a successful sudo on a RHEL/Amazon Linux box?
questions.why: On RHEL-family (incl. Amazon Linux), auth events go to /var/log/secure. Debian/Ubuntu uses /var/log/auth.log. On systemd, journalctl _COMM=sudo works on both.

questions.q: What's the practical difference between SELinux Enforcing and Permissive mode?
options: Permissive logs denials but doesn't block; Enforcing blocks and logs
Enforcing requires a reboot to change; Permissive doesn't
Permissive is for kernel modules; Enforcing is for userspace
questions.why: Permissive = log-only (great for debugging policy). Enforcing = log + block. Disabled = SELinux off entirely (different from permissive). setenforce 0 drops to permissive at runtime.

questions.q: An attacker has a low-priv shell. Which capability on a binary is essentially game over?
questions.why: CAP_SYS_ADMIN is sometimes called 'the new root' — it grants such a wide range of admin operations (mount, swapon, namespace creation, etc.) that having it on a binary is effectively root. cap_dac_override is also brutal — bypasses file permission checks.

questions.q: What does chmod 777 /tmp on a fresh box overlook?
options: The sticky bit (1777) — without it, anyone can delete anyone's files
questions.why: /tmp should be 1777 — the leading 1 is the sticky bit, which restricts deletion to the file owner. Without it, any user could delete any other user's files in /tmp.

questions.q: You see this in ~/.ssh/authorized_keys: command="/bin/bash" no-pty ssh-rsa AAAA.... What's worth flagging?
options: Nothing, this is a normal SSH key
command="/bin/bash" overrides the user's shell — common for backdoor persistence
questions.why: command="..." forces a specific command on connect — legit for restricted keys, but also a classic persistence trick for attackers, who pair it with no-pty and other options to fly under the radar.

questions.q: What's in /proc/[pid]/maps?
options: The process's memory map — every loaded library, heap, stack
questions.why: /proc/[pid]/maps is the live memory layout for a process. Useful for reverse engineering, ASLR analysis, and locating injected code. /proc/[pid]/fd is open files; /proc/[pid]/net/tcp for sockets.

questions.q: In The Matrix, what color pill does Neo take?
questions.why: Red pill — see how deep the rabbit hole goes. Blue pill = wake up in bed, believe whatever you want.

questions.q: What is HAL 9000's signature line in 2001: A Space Odyssey?
options: I'm sorry, Dave. I'm afraid I can't do that.
questions.why: HAL refusing to open the pod bay doors — possibly the most quoted AI-villain line in cinema. Fun trivia: shift each letter of 'HAL' forward by one and you get 'IBM'. (Arthur C. Clarke insisted that was a coincidence.)

questions.q: In Star Wars, what does R2-D2 carry that kicks off the whole plot?
options: Both the Death Star plans AND Leia's message
questions.why: Both — R2 is hand-delivering Leia's plea to Obi-Wan AND the stolen Death Star schematics. The original 'sneakernet exfiltration' plot device.

questions.q: Blade Runner's replicant-detection test is called what?
questions.why: Voight-Kampff — measures empathic response to provocative questions. Used by Blade Runners to identify replicants who pass as human.

questions.q: In Star Trek, what's the standard greeting from a Vulcan?
questions.why: 'Live long and prosper.' Spock's signature. Paired with the Vulcan salute — the hand sign Leonard Nimoy borrowed from a Jewish priestly blessing he saw as a kid.

questions.q: What movie's villain becomes self-aware, takes over the U.S. nuclear arsenal, and tries to wipe out humanity?
questions.why: The Terminator — Skynet goes live August 29, 1997, becomes self-aware at 2:14 AM, and launches the nukes when humans try to pull the plug. The original AI alignment failure.

questions.q: In Back to the Future, what speed does the DeLorean need to reach to time travel?
questions.why: 88 miles per hour, with 1.21 gigawatts. Doc picked 88 because it 'looked cool on the speedometer.'

questions.q: What franchise gave us 'I'll be back'?
questions.why: The Terminator (1984). Schwarzenegger originally wanted 'I will be back' (more robotic), but James Cameron insisted on the contraction. AFI ranks it among the top 50 movie quotes ever.

questions.q: In Tron, programs inside the computer call their human creators by what name?
questions.why: Users — programs inside the system worship and obey their Users (the people who wrote them). The film's whole premise. Also where the Linux /usr ambiguity probably should have gone, instead of 'Unix System Resources.'

questions.q: Which sci-fi movie features sentient machines that grow humans in pods to use as batteries?
questions.why: The Matrix. Note: this premise makes basically zero thermodynamic sense (you'd get more energy by feeding the humans directly to a generator), but it's iconic anyway. Allegedly the original script had humans-as-distributed-CPU, which would have been more accurate but harder to film.

questions.q: What does C-3PO say his function is in Star Wars?
options: Protocol droid, fluent in over six million forms of communication
Diplomatic translator for the Galactic Senate
questions.why: Protocol droid, fluent in over six million forms of communication. R2-D2 is the astromech. C-3PO complains about literally everything for nine films straight.

questions.q: In Dune, what's the most valuable substance in the universe?
questions.why: Spice / melange — found only on Arrakis. Enables faster-than-light navigation, extended life, and prescience. 'He who controls the spice controls the universe.'

questions.q: In Ex Machina, what's the name of the AI that Caleb is brought in to evaluate?
questions.why: Ava. Designed by reclusive tech CEO Nathan, who tasks Caleb with running a Turing-test variant on her. Spoiler: Ava passes the test in ways nobody expected.

questions.q: In Star Trek, what does the captain say to start the warp drive?
questions.why: 'Engage.' Picard's signature command, usually paired with a finger-point at the viewscreen. 'Make it so' is what he says when delegating an order to Riker.

questions.q: What's the name of the dystopian megacorp in RoboCop (1987)?
questions.why: OCP runs Detroit. Weyland-Yutani is Alien, Cyberdyne is Terminator, Tyrell makes the replicants in Blade Runner. All four are perfect cyberpunk-villain-corp names.

questions.q: What was Anaconda Inc. originally called?
questions.why: Anaconda Inc. was originally Continuum Analytics. Conda was derived and created from Continuum members.

questions.q: "Be the kitten" — what does this internal Anaconda phrase mean?
options: Approach change with curiosity instead of fear
A code name for a stealth product
questions.why: From the April 2022 All-Hands — Peter talked about change in the context of fear vs. curiosity. The kitten shows up at 8:55 in the recording.

questions.q: The Anaconda "defaults" channel is actually three channels. Which three?
questions.why: Defaults = main (general), r (R packages), and msys2 (Windows libraries).

questions.q: What is "Perseverance" inside Anaconda?
options: The team in the Distribution org that builds and maintains Defaults
The internal name for Package Security Manager
questions.why: Perseverance is the team that maintains the Defaults repository — CVE curation, marquee packages, customer-requested builds.

questions.q: What does conda's name actually come from?
options: It's an acronym (Cross-platform Open-source Native Distribution Application)
questions.why: Just a shortening of 'Anaconda'. No fancy acronym. Conda is itself open source with community governance — separate from the Anaconda Distribution.
